attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital for management of an infected ulcer on the sole of her right foot. This is prior to a planned revascularisation procedure. Whilst being immobilised in bed she is put on subcutaneous heparin as a thromboprophylaxis measure. She has a medical history of type 2 diabetes. Her medications includes metformin. She has no known drug allergies.

On her admission examination she appears well. Her pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 142/92mmHg, and temperature 37.2°C. There is a necrotic ulcer over the first metatarsal head.

On admission all her haematological and biochemical values were within normal range, apart from an elevated white cell count. One week after admission her platelet count is 83 x 109/L (150 – 450).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Cease heparin and apply calf-compression device
attributes.explanation:
This patient has developed heparin-induced thrombocytopaenia (HIT) - an uncommon, but serious side effect of heparin. HIT is more commonly seen in the administration of unfractionated than low molecular weight heparin. It is seen more often in women than men and is associated with a 5%-10% mortality. Characteristically, the thrombocytopaenia becomes evident five to ten days after starting the heparin. Patients with suspected HIT can be evaluated using the 4Ts clinical scoring system; see (2) for further reading.

These patients are at high risk of a thrombotic event and although heparin should be ceased some form of prophylaxis must be continued. Changing to a low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin) would not be appropriate as this is also associated with HIT and there is potential for cross-reactivity. In many other circumstances some form of sequential-compression device could be considered, but in patients with limb ischaemia, use of such pressure devices could be complicated by tissue necrosis. Warfarin should not be used as a semi-urgent surgical procedure has been planned and the use of warfarin as the initial anticoagulant in HIT carries the risk of skin necrosis.

Current guidelines from the Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand recommend the use of non-heparin anticoagulants in HIT; danaparoid, argatroban, fondaparinux and bivalirudin. Of these, danaparoid and fondaparinux are associated with lower bleeding rates. Anticoagulation should be continued until platelet levels reach >150 x 109/L. In patients who experience thrombosis with HIT, three months of anticoagulation is recommended, which can be achieved by transitioning to warfarin or a direct oral anticoagulant. The patient should be advised to avoid heparin in future.
learningPoints: Heparin-induced thrombocytopaenia (HIT) most commonly becomes apparent five to ten days after starting heparin.
For patients with HIT, the heparin should be ceased and guidelines recommend non-heparin anticoagulants as replacement (e.g. danaparoid, fondaparinux).
references: Prince M, Wenham T. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Postgrad Med J 2018;94:453-57. Available at: https://academic.oup.com/pmj/article/94/1114/453/6958988?login=true

Warkentin T. Clinical picture of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) and its differentiation from non-HIT thrombocytopenia. Thromb Haemost. 2016;116(5):813-822. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27656712/

Gottlieb A, Tabares A, Levy P, Sprung J, Kottke-Marchant K. Use of Low Molecular Weight Heparin in Patients with Heparin-induced Thrombocytopenia Undergoing Carotid Endarterectomy. Anaesthesiology. 1996;85:678-681. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8853099/

Srinivasan A, Lawrence R, Bartholomew J. Warfarin-Induced Skin Necrosis and Venous Limb Gangrene in the Setting of Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia. Arch Intern Med. 2004;164(1):361-369. Available at: https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamainternalmedicine/article-abstract/216469

Joseph J, Rabbolini D, Enjeti A, Favaloro E, Kopp M, McRae S, et al. Diagnosis and management of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia: a consensus statement from the Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand HIT Writing Group. Med J Aust. 2019;210(11):506-516. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31155728/
